records.name: Public Testimonies
records.description: Truth and Reconciliation Commission: Victims of apartheid publicly sharing their traumas to force national reckoning.

records.name: Community Deputations
records.description: The Salt March: Gandhi sending a formal letter/delegation to the Viceroy before the march began.

records.name: Door-to-Door Petitions
records.description: Chartist Movement: Working-class activists gathering millions of signatures in the 1830s to demand voting rights.

records.name: Targeted Leafleting
records.description: The White Rose: Students risking their lives to distribute anti-Nazi leaflets at the University of Munich.

records.name: Mock Awards
records.description: Billionaires for Bush: Satirical street theater group giving fake "awards" to politicians to highlight corporate lobbying.

records.name: Neighborhood Picketing
records.description: Delano Grape Strike: Farmworkers picketing outside local grocery stores to inform consumers of labor abuses.

records.name: Wearable Symbols
records.description: Madres de Plaza de Mayo: Mothers wearing white headscarves while protesting the "disappearance" of their children.

records.name: Protest Disrobings
records.description: Women of Liberia Mass Action: Leymah Gbowee and others threatening to strip naked to shame warlords into peace talks.

records.name: Destruction of Property
records.description: Draft Card Burnings: Anti-Vietnam War activists publicly burning their conscription cards.

records.name: Synchronized Lighting
records.description: Cacerolazos: Flickering house lights alongside banging pots to signal neighborhood defiance of the Pinochet curfew.

records.name: Washable Paint
records.description: Las Tesis: Feminist collectives using paint and chalk on streets to demarcate zones before their synchronized protests.

records.name: Street Renaming
records.description: Black Lives Matter Plaza: Activists (and eventually the city) renaming the street leading to the White House.

records.name: Community Vigils
records.description: Monday Demonstrations: Peaceful, candlelit gatherings at St. Nicholas Church that grew into mass resistance.

records.name: Guerrilla Theater
records.description: Bread and Puppet Theater: Using massive, handmade puppets in public squares to protest war and capitalism.

records.name: Disruptive Choirs
records.description: Singing Revolution: Hundreds of thousands gathering to sing banned national hymns to defy Soviet occupation.

records.name: Neighborhood Marches
records.description: Selma to Montgomery: A localized, regional march that visually demonstrated the geographic scale of disenfranchisement.

records.name: Community Teach-ins
records.description: University of Michigan Teach-ins: Faculty and students taking over halls to educate locals on the Vietnam War.

records.name: Banners & Street Art
records.description: Greenpeace Bridge Drops: Hanging massive environmental protest banners from highly visible local infrastructure.

records.name: Hyper-Local Boycotts
records.description: Montgomery Bus Boycott: A 381-day localized refusal to ride segregated city buses, crippling the local transit economy.

records.name: Producers' Boycotts
records.description: Cocoa Hold-Ups: Local farmers refusing to sell cocoa beans to British corporate monopolies until prices were fair.

records.name: Rent Strikes
records.description: Glasgow Rent Strikes: Mary Barbour leading working-class women to refuse rent payments to exploitative landlords during WWI.

records.name: Social Ostracism
records.description: Captain Boycott: The original namesake; local Irish tenants entirely shunning an abusive English land agent.

records.name: Local Student Walkouts
records.description: Soweto Uprising: Thousands of Black students walking out of schools to protest the forced use of Afrikaans.

records.name: Resignation from Boards
records.description: Finnish Senate Resignations: Mass administrative resignations to protest the "Russification" policies of the Tsar.

records.name: Neighborhood Stay-at-Homes
records.description: "Ghost Town" Protests: Anglophone regions shutting down entirely, with residents refusing to leave homes on specific days.

records.name: Sanctuary Spaces
records.description: Sanctuary Movement: Local churches openly sheltering Salvadoran and Guatemalan refugees from deportation.

records.name: Merchants' General Strikes
records.description: Hartal: A mass closure of all local shops and businesses, a tactic heavily utilized by Gandhi against British rule.

records.name: Refusal to Pay Fees
records.description: Poll Tax Rebellion: Millions of citizens refusing to pay Margaret Thatcher's localized, flat-rate community charge.

records.name: Community Divestment
records.description: Apartheid Divestment: Local university students forcing their boards to pull funds from banks dealing with South Africa.

records.name: Boycott of Local Elections
records.description: Kosovo Albanian Boycotts: Albanians entirely boycotting Serbian state elections in the 1990s to delegitimize the regime.

records.name: Refusing Local Agencies
records.description: Zapatista Autonomy: Indigenous communities openly refusing to use state courts or schools, relying solely on their own.

records.name: Ignoring Appointments
records.description: Norwegian Teachers' Strike: Teachers refusing to implement Nazi curriculum or acknowledge fascist schoolboard appointees.

records.name: Malicious Compliance
records.description: Work-to-Rule: Railway workers doing their jobs so exactly by the safety manual that trains are massively delayed.

records.name: Nonobedience Without Supervision
records.description: Everyday Resistance: Enslaved people or oppressed workers deliberately working slowly the moment the overseer looked away.

records.name: Open Violation of Ordinances
records.description: Food Not Bombs: Activists openly defying municipal bans on feeding unhoused people in public parks.

records.name: Refusal to Disperse
records.description: Tiananmen Square: Students refusing to leave the plaza despite repeated, escalating warnings from the military.

records.name: Hiding Targeted Neighbors
records.description: The Underground Railroad: A decentralized network of safe houses hiding enslaved people escaping to the North.

records.name: Turning in Fake Documents
records.description: Belgian Forgery Rings: Resistance members flooding the Nazi bureaucracy with fake IDs to protect Jewish neighbors.

records.name: The Sit-in
records.description: Greensboro Sit-ins: Black college students sitting at segregated Woolworth's lunch counters and refusing to move.

records.name: The Stand-in
records.description: Stand-ins at Movie Theaters: Civil rights activists standing in ticket lines of segregated theaters to block business.

records.name: The Ride-in
records.description: Freedom Riders: Activists riding interstate buses into the segregated South to challenge non-enforcement of civil rights laws.

records.name: The Read-in / Pray-in
records.description: Tougaloo Nine: Black students entering a segregated public library in Mississippi and quietly reading books.

records.name: Physical Obstruction
records.description: Chipko Movement: Rural villagers physically hugging trees to prevent commercial loggers from cutting down local forests.

records.name: Guerrilla Land Occupation
records.description: Landless Workers' Movement / MST: Mass, unauthorized occupations of unused agricultural land by poor families.

records.name: Overloading Facilities
records.description: Jail-No-Bail: Civil rights activists intentionally filling local jails to capacity to overwhelm the municipality's budget.

records.name: Administrative Squatting
records.description: Dakota Access Pipeline Protests: Water protectors occupying administrative offices of the Army Corps of Engineers.

records.name: Flash Mobs
records.description: Fossil Free Protests: Activists suddenly performing dramatic "die-ins" in the halls of the British Museum to protest oil sponsorships.

records.name: Parallel Neighborhood Watches
records.description: Community Alert Patrol: Preceding the Black Panthers, locals in Watts organized unarmed patrols to monitor police activity and ensure neighborhood safety.

records.name: Local Time Banks
records.description: Fureai Kippu: A decentralized system where people earn "time credits" by caring for the elderly, which they can save for their own future care or transfer to relatives.

records.name: Mutual Aid Pantries
records.description: COVID-19 Community Fridges: Decentralized, citizen-plugged-in refrigerators on sidewalks that provided 24/7 free food when government aid stalled.

records.name: Community Assemblies
records.description: Asambleas Barriales: Following the 2001 economic collapse, neighbors formed direct-democracy street assemblies to manage local resources and mutual aid.

records.name: Tactical Urbanism
records.description: Ciclovía: Originally a grassroots takeover of Bogotá's streets by cyclists, forcing the city to officially ban cars on major roads every Sunday.

records.name: Local Skill-Shares
records.description: Greek Solidarity Clinics: Following the 2008 financial crash, unemployed doctors and citizens pooled medical skills and supplies to treat uninsured locals for free.

records.name: Alternative Media
records.description: Samizdat: The decentralized, clandestine copying and distribution of banned literature and news by everyday citizens to bypass state censorship.

records.name: Public Hunger Strikes
records.description: Farmworker Fasts: Cesar Chavez famously fasting for 25 days to recommit the UFW to nonviolence and morally compel growers to negotiate.

records.name: Autonomous Encampments
records.description: ZAD de Notre-Dame-des-Landes: A decade-long "Zone to Defend" where activists occupied farmland to stop an airport, building a functional, self-governing village.

records.name: Targeted Blockades
records.description: Fairy Creek Blockade: Indigenous land defenders and allies creating complex road blockades and lock-ons to halt old-growth logging on Vancouver Island.

records.name: Independent Audits
records.description: MKSS Social Audits: Peasant workers holding public hearings to read aloud official wage records, exposing mass corruption and demanding stolen funds back.

records.name: Refusal of Enforcement
records.description: Refuseniks: Soldiers and reservists publicly refusing to serve in the occupied territories, citing systemic human rights abuses.

records.name: Alternative Economies
records.description: Cecosesola: A massive network of working-class cooperatives that bypasses state and corporate systems to provide affordable food and healthcare to hundreds of thousands.

records.name: Occupation of Power
records.description: Sunflower Student Movement: Students nonviolently occupying the national legislature for 23 days to halt a secretive trade pact with China.

records.name: Restorative Justice
records.description: Navajo Peacemaking Courts: Indigenous revitalization of community-led dispute resolution focused on healing and restitution rather than state-sanctioned punishment.

records.name: Symbolic Reparations
records.description: Shuumi Land Tax: Non-Indigenous residents in the Bay Area voluntarily paying a "tax" to the Sogorea Te' Land Trust to support the return of Indigenous land.

records.name: Resistance Training
records.description: Otpor!: A youth movement that systematically trained thousands of citizens in nonviolent resistance tactics, ultimately toppling Slobodan Milošević.

records.name: Networked Alliances
records.description: Solidarity: A decentralized trade union that linked localized factory strikes into a massive, parallel societal network that the Soviet bloc couldn't dismantle.

records.name: Yarn Bombing
records.description: Knitta Please: Originating in Texas, knitters covering cold urban infrastructure (poles, buses) to reclaim sterile, corporatized public space for community art.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Pop-up Poetry
records.description: Tahrir Square Spoken Word: Poets performing impromptu verses amidst the 2011 revolution to articulate the crowd's demands and maintain morale.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Shadow Protests
records.description: El Siluetazo: Protesters tracing life-sized human outlines on paper and plastering them on walls to represent the thousands "disappeared" by the junta.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Noise Parades (Cacerolazo)
records.description: Pots and Pans Protests: Following the 2008 financial crash, citizens banged cookware outside parliament continuously until the government resigned.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Culture Jams
records.description: Adbusters: Activists altering corporate billboards and advertisements to reveal the environmental or social damage caused by the brands.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Guerrilla Gardening
records.description: Green Guerrillas: Residents in 1970s NYC throwing seed bombs over fences and taking over abandoned, trash-filled lots to create community gardens.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Seed Bombing
records.description: Masanobu Fukuoka's Methods: The pioneer of natural farming used clay seed balls to re-vegetate desertified land without tilling or heavy machinery.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Pavement Galleries
records.description: Madonnari Tradition: The historical practice of street chalk art, frequently adapted by modern activists to bypass exclusive galleries and elite messaging.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Craftivism
records.description: Arpilleras: Women sewing scraps of clothing into vibrant, patchwork tapestries that secretly depicted the human rights abuses of the Pinochet regime.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Projection Bombing
records.description: The Illuminator: Activists projecting the "99%" Bat-Signal onto the Verizon building during Occupy Wall Street, bypassing physical access restrictions.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Protest Origami
records.description: Sadako's Cranes: A young Hiroshima victim folding 1,000 paper cranes, cementing the origami crane as a global, grassroots symbol of nuclear disarmament.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Traveling Zines
records.description: Riot Grrrl Movement: Hand-photocopied, decentralized mini-magazines passed hand-to-hand to build a feminist punk counterculture bypassing mainstream media.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Reclaimed Monuments
records.description: Edward Colston Statue: Protesters toppling a slaver's statue and artists secretly replacing it overnight with a statue of a Black Lives Matter protester.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Local Subvertising
records.description: Brandalism: Activists using fake utility keys to unlock bus stop ad cases across Paris during COP21, replacing 600 corporate ads with climate art.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Mourning Ribbons
records.description: White Ribbons for Peace: A decentralized, low-barrier way for neighborhoods to display solidarity against local or state violence.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Pop-up Bike Lanes
records.description: SF Transformation Action Group: Anonymous locals gluing traffic cones to the street overnight to force the city to protect cyclists from traffic.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Crosswalk Painting
records.description: Crosswalk Collectives: Frustrated pedestrians painting high-visibility zebra crossings at dangerous intersections where the city refuses to act.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Parklet Creation
records.description: PARK Day (USA): Feeding a parking meter and unrolling turf, a bench, and a potted plant to prove the space is better used for people than idle cars.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Unofficial Wayfinding
records.description: Walk [Your City]: A resident printing and hanging signs saying "It's a 5-minute walk to the library," increasing local foot traffic and community connection.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: De-paving Parties
records.description: Depave Portland: Community groups bringing sledgehammers to unneeded parking lots to break up asphalt and restore the earth's natural water absorption.
records.type: art, public space, and urban hacking

records.name: Neighborhood Composting
records.description: Havana's Organopónicos: Following the Soviet collapse, neighborhoods collectively turned vacant lots into organic farms and compost hubs to survive the food crisis.

records.name: Tool Libraries
records.description: Berkeley Tool Lending Library: Starting out of a garage, a community pooled resources so neighbors didn't have to individually buy expensive tools.

records.name: Tree Sitting
records.description: Julia Butterfly Hill: Living in a 1,000-year-old redwood tree named Luna for 738 days to successfully prevent Pacific Lumber from clear-cutting the area.

records.name: Water Testing
records.description: Flint Citizen Science: Ignored by local government, residents partnered with independent scientists to test and prove the toxicity of their own municipal water.

records.name: Human Chain for Safe Passage
records.description: Stop de Kindermoord: In the 1970s, parents blockaded streets and formed human chains to protect kids from cars, leading to modern Dutch cycling infrastructure.

records.name: Open-Air Living Rooms
records.description: Reclaim the Streets: Activists hijacking major roads, blocking cars with tripods, and throwing massive, impromptu street parties with couches and sound systems.

records.name: Pothole Planting
records.description: The Pothole Gardener: Planting miniature gardens inside neglected municipal potholes to draw media attention to crumbling local infrastructure.

records.name: Energy Co-ops
records.description: Schönau Power Grid: After Chernobyl, local parents raised funds, formed a co-op, and successfully bought their town's energy grid to force a switch to renewables.

records.name: "Take a Seat" Campaigns
records.description: William Whyte's Studies: Activists putting out unauthorized folding chairs in sterile corporate plazas to reclaim them as public gathering spaces.

records.name: Guerrilla Lighting
records.description: Nightwalks: Women's groups installing temporary, battery-powered lights in dark alleys to highlight the city's failure to provide safe infrastructure.

records.name: Reclaiming Vacant Lots
records.description: People's Park: Thousands of Berkeley residents turning a muddy, university-owned lot into a community park, defending it heavily against police reclamation.

records.name: Rainwater Catchment
records.description: Rajendra Singh's Johads: A grassroots effort organizing villages to build traditional earthen dams, bringing rivers back to life and bypassing state water mismanagement.

records.name: Foraging Maps
records.description: Falling Fruit: A massive, open-source collaborative map created by locals pointing out edible fruits and nuts growing in public urban spaces.

records.name: Cooling Centers
records.description: Chicago Heat Wave Mutual Aid: When the city failed, localized networks of neighbors checking on the elderly and opening basements saved hundreds of lives.

records.name: Walking School Buses
records.description: Safe Routes to School: Parents organizing walking convoys to get kids to school safely, directly reducing car dependency and protesting dangerous road design.

records.name: Clean-Air Monitoring
records.description: AirBox Project: Citizens deploying cheap, open-source air sensors to bypass state data and prove local industrial pollution levels.

records.name: Gleaning Networks
records.description: Les Glaneurs: A historical, decentralized practice (protected by old laws) where the poor harvest leftover crops from commercial fields, reborn today as mutual aid.

records.name: Crop Swaps
records.description: LA Crop Swap: Neighbors exchanging surplus backyard lemons, avocados, and herbs to reduce dependency on corporate grocery chains.

records.name: Anti-Hostile Architecture
records.description: Space, Not Spikes: Activists placing mattresses and mini-libraries over metal spikes installed by luxury apartments to deter unhoused people from sleeping.

records.name: Flash Mob Cleaning
records.description: Trashero / #TrashTag: Mass crowds coordinating online to suddenly converge on heavily polluted local beaches or parks, clearing them in hours.

records.name: Free Stores
records.description: The Diggers: An anarchist improv group in 1960s San Francisco running entirely free stores to prototype a post-capitalist society.

records.name: Really Really Free Markets
records.description: Anti-Globalization Movement: Hosting festival-like days in public parks where goods, food, and haircuts are shared with no money or barter allowed.

records.name: Seed Libraries
records.description: Navdanya: Vandana Shiva’s network creating community seed banks to protect local biodiversity from corporate patents (like Monsanto).

records.name: Neighborhood Carpools
records.description: Montgomery Dispatch System: To sustain the bus boycott, Black churches organized a highly complex, free alternative transit system with dispatchers and routing.

records.name: Local Currency / Scrip
records.description: Ithaca Hours: A widely used local currency in upstate New York that kept wealth circulating strictly within local businesses and local workers.

records.name: Pay-What-You-Can Cafes
records.description: SAME Café: Restaurants operating without set prices, where patrons pay by donating money or washing dishes for an hour.

records.name: Local Debt Jubilees
records.description: Rolling Jubilee: Activists crowdfunding to buy local medical or student debt for pennies on the dollar on the secondary market, and then legally canceling it.

records.name: Skill Auctions (LETS)
records.description: Local Exchange Trading Systems: A mutual credit system where a plumber fixes a leak in exchange for credits they use to get a haircut locally.

records.name: Neighborhood Bulk Buying
records.description: Seikatsu Club: Housewives pooling money to buy wholesale, direct from farmers to bypass high retail prices and demand pesticide-free food.

records.name: Worker Co-op Incubation
records.description: Mondragon Corporation: Starting with a single small parish in the Basque region, locals built the world's largest federation of worker-owned co-ops.

records.name: Community Land Trusts
records.description: New Communities Inc.: Born from the Civil Rights movement, Black farmers collectively buying land to remove it from speculative markets permanently.

records.name: Repair Cafes
records.description: Amsterdam Repair Café: Local tinkerers gathering to fix neighbors' broken toasters and clothes for free to fight planned obsolescence.

records.name: Childcare Co-ops
records.description: Socialist Creches: Women’s liberation groups in the 1970s organizing shared, rotating childcare to free each other up for work or activism.

records.name: Rent Parties
records.description: Harlem Renaissance: Black tenants hosting jazz and dance parties with a small entry fee to cover extortionate, discriminatory rents in 1920s New York.

records.name: Free Laundromat Days
records.description: Laundry Love: Mutual aid groups buying out a local laundromat’s machines for an evening so low-income neighbors can wash clothes for free.

records.name: Solidarity Deliveries
records.description: COVID-19 Mutual Aid: Neighborhood WhatsApp groups rapidly self-organizing to deliver groceries and prescriptions to immunocompromised locals.

records.name: Street Pantries
records.description: Little Free Pantries: Evolving from "Little Free Libraries," these are small, unmonitored wooden boxes on posts providing 24/7 non-perishable food.

records.name: Community Fridges
records.description: Foodsharing.de "Fairteiler": Installing public refrigerators where individuals and bakeries drop off surplus food to prevent waste and feed neighbors.

records.name: "Pay It Forward" Chains
records.description: Caffè Sospeso: The Neapolitan working-class tradition of paying for two coffees—one for yourself, and a "suspended" one for the next person who can't afford it.

records.name: Squatter Support Networks
records.description: Hafenstraße: Neighborhoods forming deep logistical networks to deliver food, barricade materials, and legal aid to radical housing squats in Hamburg.

records.name: Pavement Chalk Debates
records.description: Occupy Wall Street: Activists writing prompts on the pavement and leaving chalk, sparking spontaneous, hours-long political debates among strangers.

records.name: "Ask Me Anything" Booths
records.description: The Human Library: Creating pop-up events where "books" are actual marginalized people (e.g., a refugee) whom locals can "borrow" for an honest 30-minute chat.

records.name: Community Archives
records.description: Palestine Oral History Project: Grassroots archiving of elder memories to preserve village histories deliberately erased by the state.

records.name: Invisible Theater
records.description: Theater of the Oppressed: Augusto Boal's method of actors staging a fake argument about inequality on a real subway to pull actual commuters into the debate.

records.name: Banned Book Read-Alouds
records.description: Reading Lolita in Tehran: Secret, localized book clubs of women gathering to read western literature strictly banned by the state.

records.name: Neighborhood Office Hours
records.description: Rojava Communes: Local, rotating delegates sitting in neighborhood centers weekly, accessible to anyone to resolve community disputes directly.

records.name: Citizen Audits
records.description: Participatory Budgeting: Starting in Porto Alegre, citizens organizing to painstakingly track municipal funds and democratically decide how the city budget is spent.

records.name: Open Mic Grievances
records.description: Speakers' Corner: The historic area in London's Hyde Park where anyone can set up a soapbox and speak their mind on local or global issues without permit.

records.name: Letter-Writing Parties
records.description: Write for Rights: Combining the administrative pressure of mass mail to officials with the social bonding of a neighborhood potluck.

records.name: Language Swaps
records.description: Voluntariat per la Llengua: Pairing native Catalan speakers with newcomers in cafes to informally teach the language and integrate them into local culture.

records.name: Walk-and-Talks
records.description: Jane’s Walks: Citizen-led walking tours honoring urbanist Jane Jacobs, focused on observing local gentrification, architecture, and community needs.

records.name: Empty Chair at the Table
records.description: Climate Change Negotiations: Activists forcing a physical, empty chair to remain at a panel to represent marginalized voices, future generations, or political prisoners.

records.name: Whistleblower Networks
records.description: Pentagon Papers Defense: Local networks passing the hat and providing social cover for Daniel Ellsberg and others leaking classified corruption.

records.name: Bumper Sticker Campaigns
records.description: End Conscription Campaign: The massive, localized distribution of anti-apartheid draft-dodging stickers that visibly overwhelmed state censors.

records.name: "Adopt-a-Politician"
records.description: Indivisible: Small local groups assigning members to meticulously track and publicize every single local town hall, vote, and donor of a specific representative.

records.name: Community Fact-Checking
records.description: g0v: Civic hackers rapidly building open-source tools to fact-check government claims and local media misinformation in real-time.

records.name: Reverse Town Halls
records.description: March for Our Lives: High school students hosting forums where they set the agenda, and politicians were invited only if they agreed to sit and listen.

records.name: Intergenerational Dialogues
records.description: Gacaca Courts: Post-genocide community gatherings led by elders, prioritizing localized truth-telling and restorative dialogue over formal state tribunals.

records.name: Community Syllabi
records.description: #StandingRockSyllabus: Activists compiling and distributing reading lists of Indigenous history so supporters could educate themselves on the deep context of the pipeline fight.

records.name: Potluck Assemblies
records.description: Free Breakfast for Children: The Black Panther Party using the provisioning of food as a central hub to politically educate and organize the neighborhood.

records.name: Disruption of Evictions
records.description: Plataforma de Afectados por la Hipoteca / PAH: Neighbors forming impenetrable human blockades at the doors of foreclosed homes to stop bank evictions.

records.name: Cop-Watching Patrols
records.description: Berkeley Copwatch: Unarmed citizens legally carrying cameras, systematically following police patrols to deter brutality and document arrests.

records.name: Sanctuary Businesses
records.description: Safe Havens: Local storefronts putting a specific decal in their window, training staff to provide emergency refuge for individuals fleeing street harassment.

records.name: Noise Blockades
records.description: Anti-Fascist Sound Systems: Activists setting up massive concert speakers to entirely drown out Neo-Nazi rallies in town squares with techno or classical music.

records.name: Banners on Refuse
records.description: Naples Garbage Crisis: Citizens protesting mafia-linked waste mismanagement by turning uncollected trash heaps into barricades and protest art canvases.

records.name: Infrastructure Watch
records.description: Map Kibera: Residents of a massive slum taking GPS devices to map their own neighborhood, forcing the government to acknowledge the need for water and clinics.

records.name: Silent Processions
records.description: Silent Parade of 1917: 10,000 Black Americans marching down 5th Avenue in total silence to protest lynchings and racial violence, an incredibly eerie and powerful tactic.

records.name: Community Mesh Networks
records.description: Guifi.net: A bottom-up, citizen-driven open telecommunications network serving tens of thousands, bypassing corporate internet monopolies entirely.

records.name: Local Pirate Radio
records.description: Radio Libre: In the 1970s, activists set up illegal, low-power FM transmitters in apartments to broadcast anti-capitalist news and evade state monopolies.

records.name: Neighborhood Podcasts
records.description: Voz das Comunidades: Favelas producing their own grassroots journalism via podcasts and apps to counter the demonizing narratives of mainstream, state-aligned media.

records.name: Distributed Wi-Fi
records.description: SNET / Street Network: Gamers and locals stringing ethernet cables across rooftops to build a massive, illegal intranet to share media and chat, bypassing the state firewall.

records.name: QR Code Bombing
records.description: Anti-Extradition Protests: Protesters plastering the city with QR codes linking to secure communications, mutual aid funds, and boycott lists.

records.name: Geo-Fenced Blasts
records.description: AirDrop Protests: Activists using Apple AirDrop on subways to anonymously mass-send uncensored news flyers to the phones of everyday commuters.

records.name: Counter-Mapping
records.description: Indigenous Land Mapping: Local tribes using drones and GPS to map their ancestral forests, using the data to legally block corporate palm oil concessions.

records.name: Sousveillance
records.description: Darnella Frazier: The organized or spontaneous act of citizens recording state authority from the "bottom up," as seen in the recording of George Floyd's murder.

records.name: Unofficial "Blue Plaques"
records.description: Guerilla Commemoration: Activists putting up official-looking historical markers on buildings to honor overlooked working-class rebels or tragic events.

records.name: Coordinated Sick-Outs
records.description: West Virginia Teachers: "Blue Flu," where massive numbers of local public workers all call in sick on the same day to paralyze a system without declaring an illegal strike.

records.name: Jury Nullification
records.description: FIJA Activists: Handing out flyers outside courthouses to educate potential jurors on their absolute right to return a "Not Guilty" verdict if they believe a law is unjust.

records.name: Over-Compliance
records.description: Checkpoint Gridlock: Workers moving through restrictive security checkpoints following every minor rule so thoroughly and slowly that the system backs up for miles.

records.name: Protective Accompaniment
records.description: Peace Brigades International: High-visibility volunteers physically walking alongside threatened union leaders or activists as living, international bodyguards.
